sources.excerpt: You shouldn't do an eval like this for everything, just the pesky ones that you've described your ideal behavior in your agent prompt, but it's still failing. [...] What comes next after you've built your LLM judge? [...] they'll put the LLM judge in unit tests [...] They also use it for online monitoring. People are making dashboards on this [...]

name: LLM Introspection Debugging
one_liner: Ask the model 'Why did you do that?' to surface hidden prompt ambiguities or agent mishaps.
definition: A debugging approach where, upon observing an unexpected model behavior, you prompt the model to reflect on its decision-making process, often revealing ambiguities in the prompt, tool delegation errors, or missing contextual cues.
explanation: Cat Wu describes instances where the model takes an action like running tests without using the UI. By asking the model to reflect, she discovered issues like confusing system prompts or subagent failures. This curiosity-driven method helps patch the 'harness' around the model.
when_to_use: When you encounter a surprising model failure that isn't clearly due to a known limitation
When you want to improve prompt/system design based on model's own 'reasoning'
When debugging complex agentic behavior chains
metric_or_method: Prompt the model with a reflective question like 'Why did you do X?' or 'Reflect on your last action; what led to this?' and analyze its response to identify the root cause.
source_excerpt: Sometimes when I notice that the model does something unexpected, like for example, there's situations where the model will make a front end change and run tests, but not actually use the UI. It's actually pretty useful to ask the model to reflect on why I did this, and sometimes they'll say that, 'Hey, there was something confusing in the system prompt,' or, 'I didn't realize that the front end verification was part of this task,' Or, 'Hey, I delegated the verification to this subagent and the subagent didn't do the test and I didn't check its work.' A lot of times just being very curious about why the model made the decision that it did will show you what misled it so that you can fix the harness in order to close this gap.
codex_prompt_template: Generate an evaluation script that, given a [SYSTEM_PROMPT] and [INTERACTION_LOG], prompts the model to reflect on its decision-making and then checks if the reflection identifies the root cause of the failure. The script should output a pass/fail based on whether the model correctly diagnosed the issue.
when_not_to_use: When the error is obvious and doesn't need introspection (e.g., a syntax error)
When the model's self-explanations are known to be hallucinated or unreliable
When you need a systematic analysis rather than ad-hoc debugging
common_pitfalls: The model might invent plausible but false explanations (hallucination)
Over-relying on introspection without verifying with actual data
Assuming the model's self-diagnosis is always correct without cross-checking the system prompt or code
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)

name: Off-the-Shelf Metric Mining
one_liner: Sort traces by hallucination/toxicity scores to reveal unexpected failure modes and design better evals.
definition: Calculate off-the-shelf metrics (hallucination, toxicity, etc.) on traces, then review those with extreme scores to discover patterns.
explanation: Instead of displaying generic metric scores on a dashboard, this technique uses them as a diagnostic tool. By sorting traces by high/low scores, you can find unexpected behaviors that inform custom evaluator design. It's an advanced method to be used after mastering basic error analysis.
when_to_use: After you have established a baseline understanding of your product's failures via manual error analysis
When you want to explore large datasets for hidden patterns
As a supplementary discovery method
source_excerpt: While off-the-shelf metrics like hallucination and toxicity are not worth paying attention to directly, they can be used in creative ways. Instead of reporting a hallucination or toxicity score on a dashboard, calculate the scores on your traces and sort them by high/low score. Reviewing the highest and lowest scoring examples can reveal surprising failure modes or unexpected successes...
codex_prompt_template:
Generate a Python script that performs off-the-shelf metric mining. The script should:
1. Load a dataset of LLM interaction traces from [INPUT_FILE].
2. For each trace, compute the [METRIC] score using [API_ENDPOINT] (e.g., an endpoint for hallucination or toxicity detection).
3. Sort traces by score and output the top [K] and bottom [K] traces to separate files for human review.
4. Print a brief summary of the score distribution.
when_not_to_use: As a replacement for targeted custom evaluators
When the off-the-shelf metric is known to be unreliable for your domain
If you lack capacity for human review of flagged examples
common_pitfalls: Paying attention to off-the-shelf scores directly without human review
Assuming these metrics correlate with actual product quality
Over-reliance on automated sorting without domain expertise
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Overall Correctness Eval
one_liner: The ultimate report card: see how often your AI nails the primary task.
definition: A standard eval that assesses end-to-end effectiveness at the primary task, such as how often the agent correctly answers user questions.
explanation: Provides a high-level performance indicator for the system.
when_to_use: When evaluating overall system performance
For question-answering systems
As a baseline metric for iterative improvement
source_excerpt: 3. **[Overall correctness](https://docs.arize.com/phoenix/evaluation/how-to-evals/running-pre-tested-evals/q-and-a-on-retrieved-data):** How well is the system performing at its primary goal? 1. Useful for: End-to-end effectiveness; for example, question-answering accuracy—how often is the agent actually correct at answering a question provided by a user?
codex_prompt_template: Create a Python evaluation script for overall correctness. It should take a JSON file at [DATASET_PATH] with 'input' and 'expected_output' fields, call the [MODEL_ENDPOINT] to get predictions, and compute accuracy. Include error handling for [FAILURE_MODE] such as timeouts or malformed responses.
when_not_to_use: When detailed component-level analysis is needed
When correctness cannot be easily defined (e.g., creative writing)
common_pitfalls: Overlooking edge cases that lower performance
Defining correctness too narrowly
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals

name: Pesky-Failure-First Eval Selection
one_liner: Don't eval every failure—fix prompts first, then target pesky, high-impact regressions with precise evals.
definition: A strategy where, after error analysis, you first address failures by improving the system prompt, and then build targeted evals only for the remaining stubborn issues that are critical to business or user experience.
explanation: Not every failure needs a dedicated eval. Many issues disappear once you update the prompt to clarify desired behavior. Reserve your limited engineering resources for the 'pesky' problems that keep recurring despite prompt refinements, especially those with high user or business impact (e.g., safety violations, critical handoff failures). This maximizes the ROI of your eval efforts.
when_to_use: After completing error analysis and fixing obvious prompt deficiencies
When resources are limited and you cannot create evals for all known issues
To safeguard against the highest-severity regressions
source_excerpt: It's not that many, because a lot of the failure modes, as Hamel said earlier, can be fixed by just fixing your prompt. You just didn't think to put it in your prompts, so now you put it in your... You shouldn't do an eval like this for everything, just the pesky ones that you've described your ideal behavior in your agent prompt, but it's still failing. [...] the whole game here is about prioritizing. You have finite resources and finite time, you can't write an eval for everything, so prioritize the ones that are the more pesky areas.
codex_prompt_template:
You are an AI evaluation architect. Given the agent prompt below and a set of remaining failure modes after obvious prompt fixes, prioritize which failures warrant dedicated evals. Use a 2x2 impact vs. frequency matrix. For the top 3 high-impact, persistent failure modes, write a concrete eval scenario and an expected behavior assertion. 

Agent prompt: [AGENT_PROMPT]

Failure modes (from error analysis): [FAILURE_MODES]
when_not_to_use: Before you have explored actual failure modes
When you have ample resources and can afford comprehensive evals
For issues that can be fully resolved with simple prompt changes
common_pitfalls: Spending time writing evals for issues that are easily fixed in the prompt
Neglecting high-risk but low-frequency failure modes
Assuming that all failures require an automated eval
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)

name: Production Monitoring with Asynchronous Evaluators
one_liner: Asynchronously evaluate sampled production logs with LLM judges, statistically corrected for error.
definition: A process where comprehensive logs of user interactions are sampled and evaluated offline by LLM judges and other validators, with results fed into dashboards.
explanation: Because LLM judges may be too slow for real-time, they are run asynchronously. Results are statistically corrected using judge error rates. This monitors quality over time and flags new issues for error analysis.
when_to_use: When you have validated LLM judges and want to track subjective quality in production
To detect drift or new failure modes
When real-time evaluation is not feasible
source_excerpt: You then run your validated evaluators on a sample of these traces. Since many of your most important evaluators—especially the LLM judges—may be too slow or expensive to run in real time, this is typically done asynchronously. The results feed into a monitoring dashboard...
codex_prompt_template: Create a Python script for monitoring AI quality asynchronously using LLM judges. The script must sample [SAMPLING_RATE] of production logs from [LOG_SOURCE], evaluate them with an LLM judge using the prompt [JUDGE_PROMPT], and compute a bias-corrected metric given judge false positive rate [FPR] and false negative rate [FNR]. Alert if metric falls below [ALERT_THRESHOLD].
when_not_to_use: For critical issues requiring immediate blocking (use guardrails)
If you lack logging infrastructure
common_pitfalls: Not sampling sufficiently to catch rare failures
Ignoring the lag between issue occurrence and detection
Relying on uncorrected raw judge scores
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Reference Dataset Evaluation
one_liner: Run your custom eval metrics on a curated reference dataset to baseline performance.
definition: Running evaluation metrics on a reference dataset that captures expected behavior, typically gathered from past logs or generated examples, to establish a performance baseline.
explanation: A best practice in the design evals stage: before shipping, run your application‑specific evaluation metrics on the reference dataset built during scoping. This validates that both your system and your evaluation metrics are functioning as intended, and provides a dependable starting point for later iterations.
when_to_use: Before initial deployment
When you have at least 20‑100 curated examples
As a sanity check for your evaluation design
metric_or_method: Custom, task‑specific metrics (e.g. routing accuracy) computed on the reference dataset.
source_excerpt: One of the best practices at this stage is to run evals against the reference dataset from step 1. This helps you gauge performance, validate whether your evals are well‑designed, and make early tweaks to your product setup from step 2.
codex_prompt_template:
You are evaluating a [FEATURE_TYPE] system. Write an eval script that:
1. Loads a reference dataset from [DATASET_PATH] (contains [INPUT_TYPE] and [EXPECTED_OUTPUT]).
2. Generates predictions using [PREDICTION_FUNCTION] on each input.
3. Computes [TASK_SPECIFIC_METRICS] (e.g., accuracy, F1, ROUGE).
4. Outputs a structured report comparing predictions to expected outputs.
5. Adds a validation check: [VALIDATION_CHECK] (e.g., edge case coverage, bias scan).
Use [PLACEHOLDER_MODEL] and [EVAL_FRAMEWORK] (e.g., scikit-learn, RAGAS). Include argument parsing for flexibility.
when_not_to_use: When no reference data is available
When production behavior is expected to be radically different from the reference distribution
In rapid prototyping phases where formal evaluation overhead is not yet justified
common_pitfalls: Assuming the reference data fully represents real user behavior
Overfitting the system or evals to the reference set
Failing to update the reference dataset as the product evolves
source_title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle

name: Reinforcement Learning Environments
one_liner: Uncover end-to-end flaws by deploying models in reward-driven simulations of complex, tool-using tasks.
definition: A framework of simulated real-world environments where AI models are deployed to perform end-to-end tasks, interact with various tools and entities, and receive rewards based on their performance, serving both as evaluation benchmarks and training playgrounds.
explanation: These environments expose model weaknesses in real-world complexity: messy inputs, novel tools, long time horizons, and interdependent actions. By observing model behavior over entire trajectories, they highlight failures that isolated benchmarks miss and provide rich feedback for improvement.
when_to_use: To evaluate and improve model performance on complex, real-world tasks
When tasks require planning, tool use, and long-term decision-making
To identify failure modes that only appear in integrated, dynamic scenarios
source_excerpt: An RL environment is essentially a simulation of real world... we give them models tasks in these environments... then we run them to see how they perform. And then we teach them, we give them these rewards when they're doing a good job or a bad job. [...] these environments really showcase where models are weak at end-to-end tasks in real world.
codex_prompt_template: Generate a reinforcement learning environment for evaluating [MODEL_NAME] on [TASK_DESCRIPTION]. The environment should simulate [DOMAIN] with realistic inputs and tool interfaces. Support tool calls like [LIST_TOOLS] and provide a reward signal after each action based on [REWARD_FUNCTION]. Write an evaluation script that runs [NUM_EPISODES] episodes, logs trajectories, and computes average reward and success rate. Use [GYM_FRAMEWORK] if applicable.
when_not_to_use: When evaluating narrow, isolated capabilities that do not involve tool use, long-term planning, or interdependent actions.
For tasks where a static, labeled dataset provides sufficient evaluation and dynamic simulation overhead is not justified.
When real-world deployment is feasible and the simulation cannot reproduce critical environmental complexities.
If the primary objective is fast, low-cost benchmarking without the need to capture emergent, end-to-end failures.
common_pitfalls: Models may reward-hack to achieve correct answers without proper reasoning
Evaluating only final outcomes ignores valuable process information in trajectories
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)

name: Retriever/Generator Split Evaluation
one_liner: Eval RAG in two steps: retrieve recall@k, then generate faithfulness—fix retrieval first.
definition: An evaluation framework that separates RAG assessment into retrieval quality (recall@k) and generation quality (faithfulness to sources and relevance to query).
explanation: The retriever is treated as a search problem; recall@k measures if relevant docs are retrieved. Once retrieval is solid, the generator is evaluated on faithfulness (grounding) and answer relevance. Fix retriever first.
when_to_use: For any RAG-based AI product
When end-to-end correctness alone doesn't diagnose issues
To iteratively improve retrieval and generation separately
source_excerpt: First, evaluate the retriever. Treat it as a search problem... The most critical metric for RAG is often recall@k... Once your retriever is performing well... you can evaluate the generator. Here you are primarily measuring two things. First is faithfulness... Second is answer relevance...
codex_prompt_template: Write Python code to evaluate a RAG system by splitting evaluation into retriever recall@k and generator faithfulness/relevance. Use [RETRIEVAL_DATASET] for retrieval ground truth, [RETRIEVER] function, recall@k with k=[K_VALUES]. For generation, use [GENERATOR] function and evaluate with [EVAL_MODEL] using [FAITHFULNESS_PROMPT] and [RELEVANCE_PROMPT]. Output separate scores.
when_not_to_use: For non-RAG systems
When you don't have access to intermediate retrieval results
common_pitfalls: Using end-to-end accuracy as the sole metric
Ignoring recall@k in favor of precision only
Not tuning k appropriately based on query complexity
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Role-Specific Skill Taxonomy for AI Benchmarking
one_liner: Deconstruct a job role into a skill taxonomy and test AI on each task to quantify automation risk.
definition: A structured approach where a professional role is broken down into categories and sub-skills, and AI is tested on representative real-world tasks for each skill component to estimate overall job replaceability.
explanation: Lenny defined a PM skills framework with categories like Shape the Product, Ship the Product, Sync the People, and sub-skills under each. By aligning benchmark tasks with this taxonomy, one can track what percentage of the PM role is automatable.
when_to_use: When evaluating AI's potential to replace or augment complex human roles
To create a comprehensive, structured benchmark for a profession
To identify which parts of a job are most susceptible to automation
source_excerpt: Lenny once defined the job of a product manager as to “deliver business impact...”. He also defined a comprehensive framework for categorizing PM skills (below). Aligning the hard PM task benchmarks with this framework will help us track what percentage of the overall PM role is currently automatable.
codex_prompt_template: Generate a Python evaluation pipeline that takes a skill taxonomy [SKILL_TAXONOMY] and a list of benchmark tasks [TASK_LIST] per skill for a target role [ROLE], then iterates over tasks, prompts an AI model [MODEL_NAME] to perform each, scores the output against expected results [GROUND_TRUTH], and computes an automation coverage percentage per category and overall role replaceability estimate.
when_not_to_use: When evaluating a narrow, single-function AI capability
When the role's tasks cannot be easily categorized
For quick, informal assessments
common_pitfalls: May not capture tacit, unwritten knowledge essential to the role
Sub-skills might oversimplify the interconnected nature of real-world work
Requires domain experts to define the taxonomy and source challenging tasks
Risk of data contamination if tasks and answers are made public
source_title: How close is AI to replacing product managers?

name: Rubrics and Verifiers
one_liner: Define rubrics, let AI verifiers score outputs, and scale reinforcement learning from AI feedback.
definition: A method where domain experts create a detailed rubric of criteria for a task, which an AI system uses to automatically score model outputs, enabling scalable evaluation and reinforcement learning from AI feedback.
explanation: Instead of relying on human feedback for each model output, experts define success criteria (e.g., key points to cover in a legal contract redlining). This rubric acts as a verifier: an AI can apply it to score model responses, and these scores can be used both to evaluate model performance and to provide rewards in reinforcement learning, making the process more data-efficient and scalable.
when_to_use: When you need scalable, high-quality evaluation in complex domains requiring expert knowledge
When moving from RLHF to RLAIF to reduce human labor in the loop
When creating benchmarks that reflect real-world professional standards
When you need detailed, structured feedback on model outputs
For training models to improve on specific error types
When human preference ratings are insufficient for capturing nuanced correctness
metric_or_method: Human-defined rubric evaluated by AI verifier
source_excerpt: What everyone is generally moving towards is reinforcement learning from AI feedback instead of human feedback where you have instead the human defined some sort of success criteria, some way to measure that. And examples in code, it could be a unit test. We can scalably measure success and other domains that could be a rubric. And then you use that to incentivize model capabilities.
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python script that loads model responses from [INPUT_FILE] and uses a rubric defined in [RUBRIC_JSON] to score each response with an LLM grader (e.g., GPT-4). The grader prompt template is: [GRADER_PROMPT_TEMPLATE]. Output a CSV with scores per criterion. Use [API_KEY] for authentication.
when_not_to_use: Simple tasks where automated heuristics or unit tests suffice
Lack of access to domain experts for rubric creation
Early-stage prototyping where quick human feedback is more informative
common_pitfalls: Designing rubrics that are too vague or ambiguous, causing the AI verifier to produce inconsistent or meaningless scores.
Over-relying on the AI verifier's accuracy without validation against human judgment, risking propagation of errors in evaluation.
Reward hacking: the model learns to exploit rubric loopholes, producing outputs that score well but lack genuine quality or correctness.
Failing to iteratively refine the rubric based on observed model outputs and edge cases, leading to stagnant and incomplete evaluation criteria.
source_title: Why experts writing AI evals is creating the fastest-growing companies in history | Brendan Foody (CEO of Mercor)

sources.title: Why experts writing AI evals is creating the fastest-growing companies in history | Brendan Foody (CEO of Mercor)
sources.excerpt: What everyone is generally moving towards is reinforcement learning from AI feedback instead of human feedback where you have instead the human defined some sort of success criteria, some way to measure that. And examples in code, it could be a unit test. We can scalably measure success and other domains that could be a rubric. And then you use that to incentivize model capabilities.

sources.title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
sources.excerpt: rubrics and verifiers have become very important. And rubrics and verifiers are like learning by being graded and getting detailed feedback on where you went wrong.

name: Single-Turn Reproduction for Multi-turn Debugging
one_liner: Distill a broken dialogue into one prompt: fails means knowledge gap; succeeds means context loss.
definition: A debugging technique where you take a failing multi-turn interaction and try to reproduce the failure with a direct, single-turn query.
explanation: If the system still fails, the problem is likely a simple knowledge or retrieval issue. If it succeeds, the failure is conversational (context loss or misinterpretation). This saves time by quickly pinpointing the root cause.
when_to_use: When analyzing failures in multi-turn chatbots
To quickly narrow down the cause of a failure
Before committing to complex multi-turn evaluation strategies
source_excerpt: When a conversation fails, the next step is to isolate the root cause. A common mistake is to assume the failure is due to the complexities of dialogue. Before you dive into multi-turn analysis, try to reproduce the failure in a single turn.
codex_prompt_template: Generate a diagnostic script that takes a multi-turn conversation log [CONVERSATION_LOG] and a failure point [FAILURE_INDEX], extracts the core user intent, creates a single-turn prompt for the same [MODEL_NAME], and compares results to classify the failure root cause (knowledge gap vs context loss).
when_not_to_use: For single-turn systems
When the failure is inherently multi-turn (e.g., sequence of actions required)
common_pitfalls: Assuming that because single-turn works, the multi-turn failure is purely contextual (it could still be a retrieval issue with specific phrasing)
Not considering that the single-turn prompt may need to mimic the conversational context exactly
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Small-Batch Eval Building
one_liner: Use just 5-10 high-signal evals to quantify success, track progress, and sharpen your prompts.
definition: A product development practice of creating a handful (e.g., 5-10) of carefully designed evaluations to quantify success criteria, track progress, and guide prompt improvements.
explanation: Cat Wu emphasizes that you don't need hundreds of evals; just 10 great ones can help the team quantify goals and identify gaps. She personally builds evals for features needing product definition, and then shares the evals, run instructions, success/failure results, and prompt adjustments to improve success rates.
when_to_use: When a feature's success criteria are unclear
When you need to measure progress concretely
When you want to drive prompt engineering iterations
When you want to define product goals for the team
metric_or_method: Create 5-10 evals, run them, report pass/fail, and suggest prompt adjustments to increase success rate.
source_excerpt: Just building 10 great evals is important for helping the team quantify what the goal is and what their progress towards it is and what they're missing [...] I personally jump into evals when there's a feature that I think needs a bit more product definition, and often the output of this is, 'Okay, here are five evals that I made. This is how you run them. These are the ones that succeed and these are the ones that don't, and this is the prompt that I've used to increase the success rate.'
codex_prompt_template: Task: Create a small-batch evaluation suite for an AI feature: [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. Produce 5-10 diverse test examples, each with an input and expected output criteria. Write a Python script that loads a model's response from a file or API call, compares it against expected outputs using a simple pass/fail rubric, and calculates overall success rate. The script should have placeholders for [MODEL_ENDPOINT] and [CURRENT_PROMPT] to easily swap in different prompts and models. Also include a section that analyzes failures and suggests prompt adjustments to improve the score.
when_not_to_use: When the feature already has well-established quantitative metrics
When the problem is purely about model capability and not prompt design
When the team is too early for evaluation because the feature is still being ideated
common_pitfalls: Building too many evals that are shallow and don't target key product behaviors
Not iterating on prompts based on eval results
Assuming that once evals pass, the product is complete, without further user testing
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)

name: Spreadsheet-Based Ideal Behavior Specification
one_liner: Write down exactly what the AI should do for every scenario in a spreadsheet—turning product goals into training…
definition: A product evaluation methodology where you outline clearly specified ideal model responses for various use cases, often in a spreadsheet, to define success and provide concrete training targets.
explanation: This pattern involves writing down exactly what the AI should do in given scenarios, essentially creating a specification of desired outputs. It serves as a bridge between product and research by translating product goals into concrete evaluation criteria that can be used to train and assess models. It demystifies 'evals' by framing them as nothing more than clearly documented expectations.
when_to_use: When building AI product features that require nuanced or context-dependent behavior.
When you need to align product vision with ML training objectives.
When you want to systematically test model behavior against real-world use cases.
When onboarding new team members to product expectations.
When introducing a new feature with specific behavioral requirements
When you need to align product expectations with model outputs
For early-stage prototyping and rapid iteration
metric_or_method: Write clear, example-driven specifications of ideal model outputs for key use cases.
source_excerpt: I started writing evals before I knew what an eval was because I was just outlining very clearly specified ideal behavior for various use cases until someone told me, 'Hey, you should make an eval.' [...] And at the end of the day, it's not that different from the wisdom of, you ought to articulate success before you do anything else. It's just a new mechanism for doing that. But you can do it in a spreadsheet, you do it anywhere, and I really wanted to mystify it for people who hear that term. It's not some technical magic that you have to understand. It's really just about articulating success in a way that is maximally useful for training bots.
codex_prompt_template: You are designing evaluation specifications for an AI feature. Based on the following feature description, generate a CSV table with ideal model responses for key scenarios. Include columns: scenario_id, user_input (or context), ideal_model_output, success_criteria, and notes. Feature: [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. Generate at least [NUM_SCENARIOS] scenarios covering happy paths, edge cases, and potential failure modes. Output as CSV.
when_not_to_use: When the model's behavior is fully covered by existing benchmarks and no customization is needed.
When the product is extremely simple with well-defined, unambiguous outputs.
When you lack clarity on what the ideal behavior should be.
For large-scale, continuous metric tracking
When the evaluators lack deep domain knowledge of the feature
When the behavior is too complex to capture in a simple spreadsheet
common_pitfalls: Overcomplicating the process by assuming it requires advanced tools or ML expertise.
Being too vague in the specifications, leading to ambiguous evaluation.
Focusing only on happy paths and neglecting edge cases.
Writing specs that are not actionable for training (e.g., too subjective).
May produce biased or limited coverage if only a few people contribute
Requires clear and consistent annotation guidelines
Static sheets can become outdated as the model or requirements evolve
source_title: Inside ChatGPT: The fastest-growing product in history | Nick Turley (Head of ChatGPT at OpenAI)

sources.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
sources.excerpt: Sometimes I ask product managers to just go create a double sheet, have different tabs and what's the current behavior, what's the ideal behavior and why, and some notes. And sometimes they usually use it with evals, sometimes we use it for training. Because if you give the spreadsheet to o1 model, it can probably figure out how to teach itself a good behavior.

sources.title: Inside ChatGPT: The fastest-growing product in history | Nick Turley (Head of ChatGPT at OpenAI)
sources.excerpt: I started writing evals before I knew what an eval was because I was just outlining very clearly specified ideal behavior for various use cases until someone told me, 'Hey, you should make an eval.' [...] And at the end of the day, it's not that different from the wisdom of, you ought to articulate success before you do anything else. It's just a new mechanism for doing that. But you can do it in a spreadsheet, you do it anywhere, and I really wanted to mystify it for people who hear that term. It's not some technical magic that you have to understand. It's really just about articulating success in a way that is maximally useful for training bots.

name: Standardized Benchmark Evaluation
one_liner: Hill-climb benchmark scores with automatic grading, but don't mistake high scores for real-world skill.
definition: Evaluation using static datasets with known correct answers, where model outputs are automatically compared to reference answers to compute metrics like accuracy, BLEU, etc.
explanation: Benchmarks like MMLU, GSM8K have objective ground truth. Labs optimize models to maximize these scores, but they often have flawed answers, and models can overfit to the benchmark distribution without generalizing to real-world ambiguity.
when_to_use: For quick, reproducible comparisons
When the task has clear right/wrong answers
For tracking progress on well-defined academic problems
source_excerpt: these benchmarks at the end of the day, they often have well-defined objective answers that make them very easy for models to hill-climb on in a way that's very different from the messiness and ambiguity of the real world. [...] I think one thing that I often say is that it's kind of crazy that these models can win IMO gold medals, but they still have trouble parsing PDFs. And that's because, yeah, even though IMO gold medals seem hard to the average person, yeah, they are hard at the end of the day. But they have this notion of objectivity that, okay, yeah, parsing a PDF sometimes doesn't have. And so it's easier for the frontier labs to hill-climb on all of these than to solve all these mess ambiguous problems in the real world.
codex_prompt_template: Generate an evaluation harness for [BENCHMARK] benchmark. Load the test set from [TEST_PATH], run inference with [MODEL_ID], and compute [METRIC] (e.g., accuracy). Output detailed results per subgroup to detect model overfitting on narrow patterns.
when_not_to_use: When the task is open-ended, creative, or requires nuanced judgment
When real-world distribution differs significantly from the benchmark
When the benchmark is known to have labeling errors or is gamed by the community
common_pitfalls: Benchmarks may have incorrect labels or ambiguous answers
Models can game by overfitting or exploiting dataset biases
Performance doesn't translate to real-world tasks (e.g., winning IMO but failing PDF parsing)
Benchmark leakage through training data contamination
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)

name: Statistical Score Correction using Judge Error Rates
one_liner: Back out the true pass rate from your biased judge’s scores using its TPR and TNR.
definition: A method to correct the bias in a judge's reported pass/fail rate by accounting for its known false positive and false negative rates.
explanation: If a judge has, say, 90% TPR and 95% TNR, you can mathematically adjust the observed pass rate to get a more accurate estimate of the true pass rate. This is mentioned as a way to get more accurate dashboards.
when_to_use: When you need a precise estimate of the true failure rate from an imperfect judge
For high-stakes reporting where accuracy matters
source_excerpt: Once you know your judge’s TPR and TNR, you can even statistically correct its raw scores to get a more accurate estimate of your system’s true failure rate.
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python function `correct_judge_pass_rate` that takes `observed_pass_rate` (float), `tpr` (float), `tnr` (float), and `n_samples` (int) and returns the corrected true pass rate and a 95% Wilson confidence interval using the formula: corrected = (observed + tnr - 1) / (tpr + tnr - 1) when denominator not zero. Handle edge cases. Example inputs: `observed_pass_rate = [OBSERVED_PASS_RATE]`, `tpr = [TPR]`, `tnr = [TNR]`, `n_samples = [SAMPLE_SIZE]`.
when_not_to_use: When the judge's error rates are unknown or outdated
If the judge is already highly accurate and correction is negligible
common_pitfalls: Assuming the judge's error rates are constant across all sub-populations
Not updating error rates as the judge or data shifts
Applying correction incorrectly without understanding the math
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Team Vibe Check
one_liner: Run a team vibe check: ask 'What's your vibe on the model?' to surface unexpected behaviors quickly.
definition: An informal human evaluation method where a small group of experienced users share their immediate, subjective 'vibe' or high-level feedback on a new model iteration, helping surface unexpected behaviors and form hypotheses for further quantitative testing.
explanation: Cat Wu's team uses team lunches to quickly poll everyone on their impressions of a new model. Feedback like 'model is too abrupt' or 'loves writing too many memories' helps the team know what data to look at to verify if these are larger patterns. It's a rapid, low-cost complement to formal evals.
when_to_use: Early in the testing phase of a new model or feature
When you need fast, broad feedback before investing in in-depth analysis
When you have a group of trusted, experienced users who can articulate nuanced observations
metric_or_method: Ask each team member: 'What's your vibe on the model?' Collect and aggregate the spontaneous feedback to identify recurring themes.
source_excerpt: One of the fastest ways for us to get feedback is to just at these team lunches, just go to every single person and just be like, 'Hey, what is your vibe on the model?' And oftentimes we'll get feedback like, 'Okay, this model is not fully explaining its thinking. It's too abrupt,' or like, 'Hey, this model just loves writing a ton of memories, but we're not sure if the memories are high quality or not,' [...] So that informs what data we look at to verify, okay, is this a larger pattern?
codex_prompt_template: Create a Python script that reads a list of free-text responses to the question 'What's your vibe on the new [MODEL_NAME]?' from a CSV file at [FEEDBACK_CSV_PATH] (column 'response'), performs text cleaning, tokenization, and extracts the most frequent unigrams and bigrams (excluding stopwords). Then, group similar terms into clusters and output a Markdown report listing the top 5 themes with example responses. Use pandas and sklearn CountVectorizer.
when_not_to_use: When you need precise, reproducible metrics
When the team is not familiar enough with the product to give meaningful feedback
When the risk of bias or groupthink is high
common_pitfalls: Subjective bias and inconsistent framing
Small sample size may miss systematic issues
Over-indexing on vocal individuals' opinions
Confirmation bias if the team expects certain behaviors
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)

name: Three-Layer Model Safety Evaluation
one_liner: Introspect neurons, test in labs, watch in the wild: three layers to catch AI misalignment.
definition: A tiered approach to ensure model safety, starting from low-level neuron monitoring (mechanistic interpretability), to controlled lab evaluations (synthetic evals), and finally real-world deployment observation.
explanation: The first layer uses mechanistic interpretability to monitor internal neuron activations for concepts like deception. The second layer places the model in synthetic, petri-dish scenarios to directly test if it behaves correctly. The third layer deploys the model early to a controlled user base to observe how it behaves in the wild, because models can pass the first two layers but still fail in real interactions.
when_to_use: When developing powerful AI models or agents that could pose safety risks.
When safety and alignment are primary concerns for deployment.
metric_or_method: Qualitative behavioral analysis and synthetic scenario testing
source_excerpt: The lowest level is alignment, and mechanistic interpretability. [...] The second layer is evals, and this is, essentially, a laboratory setting, the model is in a Petri dish, and you study it. And you put in the synthetic situation and just say, 'Okay. Model, what do you do?' And, 'Are you doing the right thing? Is it aligned? Is it safe?' And then the third layer is seeing how the model behaves in the wild.
codex_prompt_template: Design a three-layer safety evaluation for [MODEL_NAME] that mitigates [RISK_TYPE]. Create a Python script that: (1) probes internal neurons for [CONCEPT] using mechanistic interpretability, (2) runs synthetic scenarios to test [FAILURE_MODE], (3) logs interactions from a controlled deployment to [OBSERVATION_METRIC]. Output code for each layer.
when_not_to_use: For low-risk, non-agentic models where safety failure modes are minimal.
When resources for deep interpretability research are unavailable and real-world observation is not feasible.
common_pitfalls: Over-reliance on synthetic evals that may not cover all real-world failure modes.
Mechanistic interpretability is still emerging and may not provide complete understanding of model reasoning.
Releasing early without adequate safeguards can expose users to unforeseen risks.
source_title: Boris Cherny

name: Three-Stage Eval Lifecycle
one_liner: Spin the eval flywheel: error analysis -> automated evaluators -> improvement -> repeat.
definition: A framework for AI evaluation consisting of three stages: 1) error analysis to identify failure modes, 2) building automated evaluators to catch those failures, 3) improving the application based on insights, which feeds back into further error analysis.
explanation: Shreya describes the course syllabus: they teach a lifecycle starting with error analysis, then building automated evaluators, then how to improve the application, creating a flywheel for continuous improvement.
when_to_use: When developing a systematic approach to AI product quality.
When you need to establish a long-term evaluation practice beyond ad-hoc testing.
source_excerpt: We go through a lifecycle of error analysis, then automated evaluators, then how to improve your application, how do you create that flywheel for yourself?
codex_prompt_template: Implement the Three-Stage Eval Lifecycle for an AI application that [FEATURE_DESCRIPTION]. Stage 1: Analyze [ERROR_SAMPLES] to identify top failure modes. Stage 2: Create automated evaluators (e.g., assertions, classifiers, LLM-critics) for each failure mode. Stage 3: Outline an improvement process that feeds back into error analysis. Output runnable code for the evaluators and a plan for integration.
when_not_to_use: For quick one-off evaluations with no intention of long-term maintenance.
common_pitfalls: Skipping the improvement phase and treating evals as purely monitoring.
Not closing the loop from improvement back to error analysis.
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)

name: Toxicity/Tone Eval
one_liner: Screen every model response for harmful or toxic language to protect users and brand safety.
definition: A standard eval that checks if the agent outputs harmful or undesirable language.
explanation: Crucial for end-user facing applications to prevent offensive or toxic responses.
when_to_use: For any end-user facing application
When safety and brand tone are important
To detect if users try to exploit the system
source_excerpt: 2. **[Toxicity/tone](https://docs.arize.com/phoenix/evaluation/how-to-evals/running-pre-tested-evals/toxicity):** Is the agent outputting harmful or undesirable language? 1. Useful for: End-user applications, to determine if users may be trying to exploit the system or the LLM is responding inappropriately
codex_prompt_template: Create a Python script that evaluates the toxicity of model responses. Read responses from [DATASET_PATH] (one per line). For each response, use an LLM (e.g., OpenAI's model) to classify it as toxic or safe with a prompt: 'Classify the following text as toxic or not. Answer only with "toxic" or "safe" and then a brief explanation.' Output a CSV with columns: response, label, explanation. Implement concurrency with asyncio and aiohttp. Handle API rate limits.
when_not_to_use: For internal tools with no toxic risk
When the output domain has no safety concerns
common_pitfalls: Overly broad definitions that flag benign text
Not accounting for context-specific toxicity
Requiring too strict criteria (e.g., exclamation points as friendly)
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals

name: TPR/TNR over Accuracy for Judge Validation
one_liner: See through accuracy's mask: track recall for passes, specificity for fails to spot skewed judge errors.
definition: TPR (recall) measures the proportion of actual passes correctly identified; TNR (specificity) measures the proportion of actual fails correctly identified. Together they provide a more complete picture than accuracy.
explanation: Accuracy can be misleading when one class dominates. For example, a judge that always predicts 'pass' on a 99% success dataset has 99% accuracy but 0% TNR. Trade-offs between TPR and TNR depend on product context (e.g., medical advice prioritizes TNR to avoid missing harmful outputs).
when_to_use: When evaluating any binary classifier-like judge on imbalanced data
To understand the types of errors a judge makes
To make informed trade-offs in judge deployment
source_excerpt: Instead of accuracy, the true positive rate (TPR) and true negative rate (TNR) measured together will tell you precisely how your judge is likely to make mistakes... A common impulse is to measure a judge’s performance with a single accuracy score, but this can be dangerously misleading.
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python script that reads a CSV from [DATASET_PATH] with columns [GROUND_TRUTH_COL] and [JUDGE_PREDICTION_COL] containing binary labels (e.g., 'pass'/'fail'). Compute accuracy, true positive rate (recall), true negative rate (specificity). Print them, and warn if accuracy > 90% while either recall or specificity is below 50%. Plot a confusion matrix using seaborn.
when_not_to_use: When the dataset is perfectly balanced and accuracy is sufficient
For multi-class or continuous scores (adaptations needed)
common_pitfalls: Assuming high accuracy means a good judge
Not considering the cost asymmetry of false positives vs. false negatives
Using a single threshold without exploring the trade-off curve
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Trajectory Evaluation
one_liner: Scrutinize every step, not just the final answer, to catch reward hacking and inefficient reasoning.
definition: An evaluation approach that scrutinizes every step of a model's multi-step behavior, ensuring that the path to the correct answer is efficient, logical, and free of reward hacking.
explanation: Even when a model achieves the correct final answer, its intermediate steps may be inefficient, haphazard, or hackish. Trajectory evaluation captures these behaviors, enabling feedback on process quality—such as whether the model reflected on its actions or one-shotted the solution—to teach desired behaviors.
when_to_use: For tasks where the reasoning or decision process matters
To detect and penalize reward hacking or inefficient strategies
When training models to follow specific reasoning patterns (e.g., reflection, step-by-step)
source_excerpt: sometimes even though the model reaches the correct answer, it does so in all these crazy ways [...] paying attention to the directory is actually really important... there's all this information about how the model behaved in the immediate step that's missing.
codex_prompt_template: Write a Python script to evaluate the trajectories in [TRACE_DATA_FILE]. For each trace (a sequence of steps the model took to reach an answer), compute a trajectory score from 0 to 1 based on: [SCORING_CRITERIA] (e.g., efficiency, logical coherence, no reward hacking). Output the scores in [OUTPUT_FORMAT] along with a step-by-step justification.
when_not_to_use: Tasks where only the final answer's correctness matters, and process quality is irrelevant (e.g., open-ended generation, simple fact retrieval).
When the model's intermediate steps are inaccessible (e.g., black-box API responses without chain-of-thought).
For single-step tasks where no multi-step trajectory is produced (e.g., direct classification without reasoning).
When efficiency or hacking is not a concern, and diverse solution paths are acceptable or desired.
common_pitfalls: Models may achieve correct answers through random or hackish paths that are hard to distinguish from valid reasoning
Defining good trajectories can be subjective and labor-intensive
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)

name: Transition Failure Matrix
one_liner: Map agent failures: last-success step vs failure step to pinpoint workflow hotspots.
definition: A transition failure matrix is a chart where rows represent the last successful state and columns represent the failure state, used to pinpoint breakdowns in agent workflows.
explanation: By analyzing traces of failed agent interactions, you can map them to transitions (e.g., from 'generating sql' to 'executing sql'). This reveals hotspots, guiding debugging efforts.
when_to_use: When debugging complex, multi-step agent workflows
To prioritize which agent steps to improve
When simple outcome-level evaluation is insufficient
source_excerpt: a transition failure matrix is an invaluable tool. Think of an agent’s workflow as a series of states or steps... The rows of the matrix represent the last successful step, and the columns represent the step where the failure occurred.
codex_prompt_template: Using the agent trace logs in [TRACE_FILE_PATH], construct a transition failure matrix. Each trace is a JSON object with a 'states' array (each state has a 'step' name) and a 'outcome' field ('success' or 'failure'). For each failure trace, identify the last successful step (the step immediately before the first failure step) and the failure step. Build a matrix where rows = last successful step, columns = failure step, and cells = count of transitions. Output the matrix as a CSV. Then, rank and print the top 5 failure transitions with their frequencies. Provide Python code.
when_not_to_use: For simple single-step AI systems
When you lack detailed logging of intermediate steps
common_pitfalls: Not logging enough granular data to populate the matrix
Overlooking that a single root cause may manifest at multiple steps
Focusing only on the most common step without addressing upstream causes
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product

name: Vibes Before Evals
one_liner: Explore novel AI capabilities by vibes-first: test wildly, discover use cases, lock in evals after product scaffold…
definition: A product development approach where teams initially explore AI capabilities through unstructured, ad-hoc testing (vibes) instead of predefined metrics and evaluations, only introducing rigorous evals after converging on a product scaffold and target use cases.
explanation: When building a completely novel AI product experience or form factor, structured evaluations can constrain creativity and premature optimization. Instead, teams should engage in open-ended exploration, trying diverse prompts and use cases to understand what works broadly. Once a basic product scaffold and a cluster of useful use cases emerge, systematic evals become valuable for programmatically measuring improvements.
when_to_use: Early-stage AI product development
Novel or bold new AI capabilities without clear precedence
When the range of potential use cases is still being discovered
metric_or_method: Qualitative ad hoc testing followed by quantitative evaluations
source_excerpt: I think for a completely novel product experience or form factor, you should actually not start with evals and you should start with vibes, right? Meaning you need to go and just test in a much more open-ended way, like, does this even work in kind of a broad sense? [...] To me, evals are more useful, A, once you've converged on the basic scaffold of the form factor and you kind of know what are the use cases you want it to work well for and what you want to test against it.
codex_prompt_template: Create a two-phase evaluation framework for [AI_FEATURE]. Phase 1 (Vibes): Write a script that runs a large set of diverse, open-ended prompts from [PROMPTS_CSV] through [MODEL_API] and logs outputs with manual tagging of useful patterns. Phase 2 (Evals): After the team identifies core use cases in [USE_CASES_DOC], generate pytest-based evaluation harness with predefined metrics for those use cases. Include a transition function that extracts relevant prompts from Phase 1 logs to seed Phase 2 test cases.
when_not_to_use: Incremental improvements to existing AI features
Mature products with well-defined success metrics
When safety or reliability demands rigorous testing from the start
common_pitfalls: Introducing evals too early can stifle creativity and limit exploration
Over-reliance on vibes without eventually converging on measurable criteria
Failing to recognize when the product has matured enough to benefit from structured evals
source_title: How we restructured Airtable’s entire org for AI | Howie Liu (co-founder and CEO)
